commands.desc: Print the current working directory's absolute path
commands.tooltip: Tells you exactly where you are in the filesystem right now, like checking your current address. The 'absolute path' starts from the root (/) of the drive.

commands.desc: Print logical path (resolves symlinks using $PWD env var)
commands.tooltip: Shows your current location using the logical (shortcut) path. Symlinks are special 'aliases' that point to real folders; -L keeps those aliases as-is instead of revealing the real location.

commands.desc: Print physical path (resolves all symlinks to real path)
commands.tooltip: Shows your current location following every symbolic link (alias) all the way to the true, physical location on disk.

commands.desc: List directory contents
commands.tooltip: Lists the files and folders inside the current directory, like opening a folder in a file explorer.

commands.desc: List in long format showing permissions, owner, size, date
commands.tooltip: Lists files with extra detail: who owns them, their permissions (who can read/write/run them), their size, and when they were last changed. -l means 'long format'.

commands.desc: List all files including hidden files (starting with .)
commands.tooltip: Lists all files, including hidden ones (those whose names start with a dot, like .bashrc). Normally these are invisible.

commands.desc: Long format listing of all files including hidden
commands.tooltip: Shows every file — including hidden ones — with full details like owner, permissions, size, and date. Combines -l (long) and -a (all).

commands.desc: Long format with human-readable file sizes (K, M, G)
commands.tooltip: Shows file details with sizes in human-friendly units (K for kilobytes, M for megabytes, G for gigabytes) instead of raw byte counts.

commands.desc: List sorted by modification time, newest first
commands.tooltip: Lists files sorted by when they were last changed, newest first — handy for finding what you just edited.

commands.desc: List sorted by modification time, oldest first (reverse)
commands.tooltip: Lists files sorted by modification time, oldest first. The -r flag reverses the default order.

commands.desc: List sorted by file size, largest first
commands.tooltip: Lists files sorted by size, largest first — useful for finding space-hungry files.

commands.desc: List directory contents recursively
commands.tooltip: Lists files in the current folder AND all subfolders, all the way down the hierarchy. Like 'Show everything inside this folder and every folder within it'.

commands.desc: List only directories in the current directory
commands.tooltip: Lists only the directories (folders) in the current location, ignoring regular files. The */ glob matches items with a trailing slash (directories).

commands.desc: List with inode numbers
commands.tooltip: Lists files showing their inode number — a unique internal ID the filesystem uses to track each file, separate from its name.

commands.desc: List one file per line
commands.tooltip: Lists one filename per line, making it easy to process the output in scripts or pipe it to other tools.

commands.desc: List with colorized output by file type
commands.tooltip: Lists files with color-coding by type (blue for directories, green for executables, etc.) to make scanning easier.

commands.desc: List only files matching a glob pattern (e.g., .txt files)
commands.tooltip: Lists only files matching the pattern *.txt — the * is a wildcard matching any characters. You can change *.txt to any pattern.

commands.desc: Change to the home directory
commands.tooltip: Takes you to your home directory (like /home/yourname), the personal folder where your files and settings live.

commands.desc: Change to the home directory using tilde shortcut
commands.tooltip: Takes you to your home directory using the ~ shortcut, which always expands to your home path.

commands.desc: Move up one directory level to parent
commands.tooltip: Moves you up one level to the parent folder. Like clicking the 'Up' arrow in a file explorer. .. always means 'the folder containing this folder'.

commands.desc: Move up two directory levels
commands.tooltip: Moves you up two directory levels in one step. Think of it as clicking 'Up' twice.

commands.desc: Switch to the previous working directory
commands.tooltip: Jumps back to the directory you were in just before the current one — like a browser's Back button for the terminal.

commands.desc: Change to an absolute path
commands.tooltip: Takes you directly to a specific folder using its full path starting from the root (/). Like typing a full street address.

commands.desc: Change to a relative path from current directory
commands.tooltip: Takes you to a folder by its path relative to where you currently are — like giving directions from your current position.

commands.desc: Push current directory to stack and cd to new path
commands.tooltip: Saves your current location to a stack (like a bookmark), then takes you to the new path. Use popd later to return.

commands.desc: Pop the top directory from stack and return to it
commands.tooltip: Returns you to the most recently bookmarked directory saved with pushd, removing it from the stack.

commands.desc: Display the directory stack
commands.tooltip: Shows the list of saved directory bookmarks (the stack built up with pushd commands).

commands.desc: Display directory stack with one entry per line and index
commands.tooltip: Shows the directory stack with each entry on its own line along with a number index, making it easier to read.

commands.desc: Clear the directory stack
commands.tooltip: Empties the entire directory stack, removing all saved bookmarks at once.

commands.desc: Display directory and file structure as a tree
commands.tooltip: Shows all files and folders in a visual tree structure, so you can see the full hierarchy at a glance.

commands.desc: Display tree limited to 2 levels deep
commands.tooltip: Shows the directory tree but only goes 2 levels deep, avoiding an overwhelming display of deeply nested folders.

commands.desc: Display only directories in tree view
commands.tooltip: Shows only directories (folders) in the tree, hiding all regular files — useful for understanding folder structure.

commands.desc: Display all files including hidden in tree view
commands.tooltip: Shows the full tree including hidden files and folders (those starting with a dot).

commands.desc: Display tree with human-readable file sizes
commands.tooltip: Shows the tree with file sizes in human-readable units (K, M, G) next to each item.

commands.desc: Display tree excluding a pattern (e.g., node_modules)
commands.tooltip: Shows the tree but skips folders matching a pattern like 'node_modules'. The -I flag means 'ignore this pattern'.

commands.desc: Create an empty file or update its timestamp
commands.tooltip: Creates a new empty file if it doesn't exist, or updates its timestamp to 'now' if it does — without changing its contents.

commands.desc: Set a specific timestamp on a file
commands.tooltip: Sets a specific date and time on a file's timestamp. The format is YYYYMMDDhhmm. Useful for testing or sorting files by date.

commands.desc: Update only the access time of a file
commands.tooltip: Updates only the access time (when the file was last read) without changing the modification time.

commands.desc: Update only the modification time of a file
commands.tooltip: Updates only the modification time (when the file was last changed) without changing the access time.

commands.desc: Create a new directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a new directory (folder) with the given name.

commands.desc: Create nested directories recursively
commands.tooltip: Creates a chain of nested directories all at once. Without -p, you'd have to create each folder one by one. -p means 'make parents as needed'.

commands.desc: Create a directory with specific permissions
commands.tooltip: Creates a directory and immediately sets its permissions. 755 means the owner can read/write/execute, and everyone else can read/execute.

commands.desc: Copy a file to a new location
commands.tooltip: Copies a file from one location to another. The original remains in place.

commands.desc: Copy a directory recursively
commands.tooltip: Copies an entire directory and all of its contents recursively. -r means 'recursive' — without it, cp refuses to copy directories.

commands.desc: Copy file preserving permissions and timestamps
commands.tooltip: Copies a file while keeping its original permissions and timestamps intact. -p means 'preserve metadata'.

commands.desc: Archive copy preserving all attributes and symlinks
commands.tooltip: Copies a directory preserving all attributes (permissions, timestamps, symbolic links). -a is 'archive mode' — the safest copy.

commands.desc: Copy only if source is newer than destination
commands.tooltip: Copies a file only if the source is newer than the destination, avoiding unnecessary overwrites. -u means 'update'.

commands.desc: Copy with verbose output showing files copied
commands.tooltip: Copies with verbose output, printing each file as it's copied so you can see what's happening.

commands.desc: Copy interactively, prompt before overwriting
commands.tooltip: Asks for confirmation before overwriting an existing file. -i means 'interactive' — a safety net against accidental overwrites.

commands.desc: Copy without overwriting existing files
commands.tooltip: Copies a file but never overwrites an existing destination file. -n means 'no-clobber'.

commands.desc: Move or rename a file
commands.tooltip: Moves a file to a new location, or renames it if you stay in the same directory. The original disappears.

commands.desc: Move interactively, prompt before overwriting
commands.tooltip: Asks for confirmation before overwriting an existing file at the destination. Safer than plain mv.

commands.desc: Move without overwriting existing files
commands.tooltip: Moves without overwriting existing files at the destination. -n means 'no-clobber'.

commands.desc: Move with verbose output
commands.tooltip: Shows each file being moved as it happens. -v means 'verbose'.

commands.desc: Remove a file
commands.tooltip: Permanently deletes a file. Unlike a GUI trash can, there's no undo — the file is gone immediately.

commands.desc: Remove a directory and its contents recursively
commands.tooltip: Deletes a directory and everything inside it. -r means 'recursive'. Be very careful — this is permanent.

commands.desc: Force remove without prompting, ignore nonexistent
commands.tooltip: Deletes a file without asking for confirmation and ignores errors if the file doesn't exist. -f means 'force'.

commands.desc: Force remove directory recursively (dangerous)
commands.tooltip: Forcefully deletes a directory and all its contents without any prompts. Extremely dangerous — double-check before running.

commands.desc: Remove interactively, prompt before each removal
commands.tooltip: Asks 'are you sure?' before deleting each file. -i means 'interactive' — useful when deleting multiple files.

commands.desc: Remove with verbose output
commands.tooltip: Prints each filename as it's deleted so you know exactly what was removed. -v means 'verbose'.

commands.desc: Create a symbolic (soft) link
commands.tooltip: Creates a symbolic link — a shortcut file that points to another file or folder. Useful for accessing the same file from multiple places.

commands.desc: Create a hard link to a file
commands.tooltip: Creates a hard link — a second name for the same file data on disk. Both names refer to identical content; deleting one leaves the other intact.

commands.desc: Force update an existing symlink to new target
commands.tooltip: Updates an existing symbolic link to point to a new target. -s makes it symbolic, -f forces the update even if a link already exists.

commands.desc: Resolve a symlink to its absolute real path
commands.tooltip: Follows a symbolic link all the way to the actual file it points to and prints the real absolute path.

commands.desc: Sync files from source to destination verbosely
commands.tooltip: Synchronizes files from a source to a destination, copying only what changed. -a preserves all attributes, -v shows progress. Much smarter than cp for large transfers.

commands.desc: Sync with compression over SSH to remote host
commands.tooltip: Syncs files to a remote server over SSH with compression (-z) to speed up transfers over slow connections.

commands.desc: Sync and delete files in destination not in source
commands.tooltip: Syncs and also removes files from the destination that no longer exist in the source — makes the destination an exact mirror.

commands.desc: Dry-run rsync to preview changes without applying
commands.tooltip: Does a 'test run' of rsync: shows what would be copied or deleted without actually doing anything. Great for checking before committing.

commands.desc: Sync showing progress for each file transferred
commands.tooltip: Shows a live progress bar for each file being transferred so you can see how long it will take.

commands.desc: Find files by name pattern in current directory tree
commands.tooltip: Searches the current directory tree for files whose names match the pattern *.txt. The dot (.) means 'start here'.

commands.desc: Find only regular files under a path
commands.tooltip: Finds only regular files (not directories, links, etc.) under the given path. -type f filters by file type.

commands.desc: Find only directories under a path
commands.tooltip: Finds only directories under the given path. -type d filters for directories only.

commands.desc: Find files modified in the last 7 days
commands.tooltip: Finds files that were modified within the last 7 days. -mtime -7 means 'modified fewer than 7 days ago'.

commands.desc: Find files larger than 10 megabytes
commands.tooltip: Finds files larger than 10 megabytes. The + means 'greater than' and M means megabytes.

commands.desc: Find empty files and directories
commands.tooltip: Finds files and directories that are completely empty — zero bytes for files, no contents for directories.

commands.desc: Find files with exact permissions 644
commands.tooltip: Finds files with exact permissions 644 (owner can read/write, others can only read). Permissions are expressed as octal numbers.

commands.desc: Find files owned by a specific user
commands.tooltip: Finds files owned by a specific user account — useful for auditing what a particular user has created.

commands.desc: Find and delete all matching files
commands.tooltip: Finds files matching the name pattern and immediately deletes them. Combine with -name for targeted cleanup.

commands.desc: Find files and execute a command on each
commands.tooltip: Finds files matching a pattern and runs a command on each one. {} is replaced by the filename, \\; ends the command.

commands.desc: Find files by name using the locate database (fast)
commands.tooltip: Quickly searches for files by name using a pre-built database (updated by updatedb), much faster than find for simple name lookups.

commands.desc: Update the locate database of file locations
commands.tooltip: Rebuilds the locate database by scanning the filesystem. Run this after creating new files so locate can find them.

commands.desc: Display detailed file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps)
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed metadata about a file: exact size in bytes, permissions, inode number, owner, and all three timestamps (created, modified, accessed).

commands.desc: Determine a file's type by inspecting its content
commands.tooltip: Figures out what type of content a file actually contains by looking inside it — not just the extension. Can reveal if a .txt is actually an image.

commands.desc: Count lines, words, and bytes in a file
commands.tooltip: Counts the number of lines, words, and bytes in a file. wc stands for 'word count'.

commands.desc: Count only the number of lines in a file
commands.tooltip: Counts only the number of lines in a file. -l means 'lines'. Useful for knowing how big a file is.

commands.desc: Count only the number of words in a file
commands.tooltip: Counts only the number of words in a file. -w means 'words'.

commands.desc: Count only the number of bytes in a file
commands.tooltip: Counts only the number of bytes (characters) in a file. -c means 'bytes'.

commands.desc: Show differences between two files line by line
commands.tooltip: Shows the differences between two files line by line, marking lines that were added, removed, or changed.

commands.desc: Show unified diff format (used in patches)
commands.tooltip: Shows file differences in 'unified' format — a standard format used in patches and code reviews, showing a few lines of context around each change.

commands.desc: Recursively diff two directories
commands.tooltip: Compares two entire directories, showing which files differ, which are only in one, and the content differences.

commands.desc: Compare two files byte by byte
commands.tooltip: Compares two files byte by byte and reports the first position where they differ — more precise than diff.

commands.desc: Apply a patch file to update a file
commands.tooltip: Applies a patch file to update a file with the changes described in the patch. Used to distribute code changes.

commands.desc: Apply patch stripping one leading directory component
commands.tooltip: Applies a patch file while stripping one leading directory component from file paths (common when patches are generated with full paths).

commands.desc: Truncate a file to zero bytes (empty it)
commands.tooltip: Empties a file instantly without deleting it — sets its size to zero bytes. The file still exists but is blank.

commands.desc: Securely overwrite and delete a file
commands.tooltip: Overwrites a file multiple times with random data before deleting it, making recovery very difficult. -u means 'unlink (delete) after shredding'.

commands.desc: Print the entire contents of a file to stdout
commands.tooltip: Prints the entire contents of a file to the screen. Short for 'concatenate'. Simple and fast for small files.

commands.desc: Print file contents with line numbers
commands.tooltip: Prints a file's contents with line numbers on the left side — helpful for referencing specific lines.

commands.desc: Show all characters including tabs (^I) and line ends ($)
commands.tooltip: Shows all invisible characters: tabs appear as ^I and line endings appear as $ so you can see exactly what's in the file.

commands.desc: Concatenate two files into a new file
commands.tooltip: Joins two files end-to-end and saves the result to a new file. The > redirects the output to merged.txt.

commands.desc: Print file contents in reverse line order
commands.tooltip: Prints a file in reverse order — the last line appears first. 'tac' is 'cat' spelled backwards.

commands.desc: View file contents with paging (scroll forward/back)
commands.tooltip: Opens a file for reading with the ability to scroll forward and backward using arrow keys or Page Up/Down. Press q to quit.

commands.desc: Open file in less and follow new content (like tail -f)
commands.tooltip: Opens a file and automatically follows it as new content is added — like watching a live log file. Press Ctrl+C to stop.

commands.desc: Open file in less and jump to end
commands.tooltip: Opens a file and immediately jumps to the end of it, so you see the most recent content first.

commands.desc: View file with forward-only paging
commands.tooltip: Like less but you can only scroll forward, not backward. An older, simpler pager.

commands.desc: Print the first 10 lines of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the first 10 lines of a file — useful for quickly peeking at the beginning of a file.

commands.desc: Print the first 20 lines of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the first 20 lines of a file. Change the number to print however many lines you need.

commands.desc: Print the first 100 bytes of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the first 100 bytes (characters) of a file — useful for checking a binary file header.

commands.desc: Print the last 10 lines of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the last 10 lines of a file — useful for seeing recent entries in a log file.

commands.desc: Print the last 20 lines of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the last 20 lines of a file. Change the number as needed.

commands.desc: Follow a file and print new lines as they are added
commands.tooltip: Watches a file in real time and prints new lines as they appear — essential for monitoring live log files. Press Ctrl+C to stop.

commands.desc: Follow by filename, retrying if file is rotated/replaced
commands.tooltip: Like tail -f but more robust: if the file gets replaced (log rotation), it reopens the new file with the same name.

commands.desc: Print the last 100 bytes of a file
commands.tooltip: Prints the last 100 bytes of a file — useful for checking the tail of binary data.

commands.desc: Open a file in the nano text editor
commands.tooltip: Opens nano, a beginner-friendly text editor. Unlike vim, it shows keyboard shortcuts at the bottom of the screen.

commands.desc: Open nano showing line numbers
commands.tooltip: Opens nano with line numbers displayed on the left side for easier navigation.

commands.desc: Open nano with automatic backup before saving
commands.tooltip: Opens nano and automatically creates a backup copy of the file before saving any changes.

commands.desc: Open nano and jump to line 50
commands.tooltip: Opens nano and immediately jumps to line 50 — great for going directly to an error location.

commands.desc: Open a file in the vi text editor
commands.tooltip: Opens vi, a classic text editor available on virtually every Unix system. Has a steeper learning curve than nano.

commands.desc: Open a file in vim text editor
commands.tooltip: Opens vim ('Vi IMproved'), a powerful text editor with syntax highlighting, plugins, and more features than vi.

commands.desc: Open vim and jump to line 50
commands.tooltip: Opens a file in vim and immediately places the cursor at line 50.

commands.desc: Open vim and jump to first occurrence of pattern
commands.tooltip: Opens a file in vim and immediately jumps to the first match of the search pattern.

commands.desc: Open vim in read-only mode
commands.tooltip: Opens a file in vim in read-only mode so you can't accidentally change it.

commands.desc: Open vim in diff mode to compare two files
commands.tooltip: Opens two files side by side in vim's diff mode to compare them visually with highlighted differences.

commands.desc: Open multiple files in separate tabs in vim
commands.tooltip: Opens multiple files in vim, each in its own tab — switch between them with gt and gT.

commands.desc: Open multiple files in horizontal splits in vim
commands.tooltip: Opens multiple files in vim with horizontal splits (one above the other) so you can see them simultaneously.

commands.desc: Open multiple files in vertical splits in vim
commands.tooltip: Opens multiple files in vim with vertical splits (side by side) for simultaneous viewing.

commands.desc: View file with syntax highlighting and line numbers (bat)
commands.tooltip: Like cat but adds syntax highlighting based on the file type and shows line numbers. Makes code much easier to read. Requires bat to be installed.

commands.desc: View file with line numbers only, no decoration
commands.tooltip: Displays a file with bat but shows only line numbers, without extra decoration like the box border.

commands.desc: Print file with syntax highlighting without paging
commands.tooltip: Shows a file with bat's syntax highlighting but doesn't use a pager — prints everything at once, like cat.

commands.desc: Read from stdin and write to both stdout and a file
commands.tooltip: Reads input and writes it to BOTH the screen and a file simultaneously. Useful in pipelines when you want to save output while still seeing it.

commands.desc: Append stdin to a file while also printing to stdout
commands.tooltip: Like tee but adds to the end of an existing file instead of overwriting it. -a means 'append'.

commands.desc: Display a hex dump of a file
commands.tooltip: Shows the raw bytes of a file in hexadecimal (base-16) format alongside the printable characters — useful for inspecting binary files.

commands.desc: Display file contents as octal/character dump
commands.tooltip: Displays a file as octal numbers and character representations — another way to inspect raw binary file contents.

commands.desc: Extract printable strings from a binary file
commands.tooltip: Pulls out any human-readable text embedded in a binary file (like an executable), making it possible to find version strings, URLs, or error messages.

commands.desc: Extract the first field from colon-delimited file
commands.tooltip: Extracts a specific field from each line of a file that uses a delimiter character (here, : for /etc/passwd). -d sets the delimiter, -f selects the field number.

commands.desc: Show colorized diff between two files
commands.tooltip: Like diff but with color highlighting to make the differences between files much easier to see at a glance.

commands.desc: Convert file encoding from UTF-8 to ASCII
commands.tooltip: Converts a file from one text encoding to another. UTF-8 supports all languages; ASCII only supports basic English characters.

commands.desc: Convert Windows CRLF line endings to Unix LF
commands.tooltip: Converts Windows-style line endings (CRLF, \\r\\n) to Unix style (LF, \\n). Windows and Unix use different invisible characters to mark line ends.

commands.desc: Convert Unix LF line endings to Windows CRLF
commands.tooltip: Converts Unix-style line endings (LF) to Windows-style (CRLF). Useful when preparing files to be opened on Windows.

commands.desc: Convert tab characters to spaces in a file
commands.tooltip: Replaces tab characters (\\t) with spaces throughout a file — useful for fixing indentation inconsistencies.

commands.desc: Convert spaces back to tab characters in a file
commands.tooltip: Replaces spaces with tab characters — the reverse of expand.

commands.desc: Number the lines of a file and print to stdout
commands.tooltip: Adds line numbers to a file's output. Similar to cat -n but with more formatting options.

commands.desc: Wrap long lines to fit within 80 characters
commands.tooltip: Inserts line breaks so that no line in the output is longer than 80 characters. Useful for terminal display.

commands.desc: Reformat paragraphs to fit within 72 character width
commands.tooltip: Reformats paragraphs of text so each line fits within 72 characters, re-wrapping text that's too long or too short.

commands.desc: Format a file for printing with headers and pagination
commands.tooltip: Formats a text file for printing with page headers and page numbers — like a print preview in the terminal.

commands.desc: Format file as a table by aligning columns
commands.tooltip: Aligns data into neat columns by detecting field boundaries — makes tabular data much easier to read.

commands.desc: Search for a pattern in a file
commands.tooltip: Searches a file for lines matching a pattern and prints them. grep stands for 'Global Regular Expression Print'.

commands.desc: Case-insensitive pattern search
commands.tooltip: Searches for a pattern without caring about uppercase/lowercase. 'Hello' would match 'hello', 'HELLO', etc.

commands.desc: Recursively search for pattern in directory
commands.tooltip: Searches every file inside a directory and its subdirectories. -r means 'recursive'.

commands.desc: List only filenames containing the pattern
commands.tooltip: Instead of printing matching lines, only prints the names of files that contain at least one match.

commands.desc: Count the number of matching lines
commands.tooltip: Counts and prints how many lines in the file match the pattern, instead of showing the lines themselves.

commands.desc: Show line numbers alongside matching lines
commands.tooltip: Shows matching lines together with their line numbers so you can find them easily in the file.

commands.desc: Invert match — show lines that do NOT match
commands.tooltip: Prints every line that does NOT match the pattern — inverts the search. -v means 'invert'.

commands.desc: Match whole words only
commands.tooltip: Matches only complete words. 'cat' would match 'cat' but not 'catch' or 'concatenate'.

commands.desc: Match entire lines only
commands.tooltip: Matches only lines where the pattern is the entire line — not just part of it.

commands.desc: Use extended regex to match multiple patterns
commands.tooltip: Uses extended regular expressions, letting you match multiple patterns with | (OR). Equivalent to egrep.

commands.desc: Use Perl-compatible regex for matching
commands.tooltip: Uses Perl-compatible regular expressions — the most powerful type, supporting advanced patterns like \\d for any digit.

commands.desc: Print only the matched portion of each line
commands.tooltip: Prints only the part of each line that matched the pattern, not the whole line.

commands.desc: Print 3 lines of context after each match
commands.tooltip: Shows the 3 lines following each match so you have context about what comes after the pattern.

commands.desc: Print 3 lines of context before each match
commands.tooltip: Shows the 3 lines before each match so you have context about what comes before the pattern.

commands.desc: Print 3 lines of context before and after match
commands.tooltip: Shows 3 lines before AND after each match — full context around every occurrence.

commands.desc: Highlight the matched pattern in color
commands.tooltip: Highlights the matched text in color, making it easy to spot within long lines.

commands.desc: Stop after finding 5 matches
commands.tooltip: Stops searching and exits after finding 5 matches. -m means 'max count'.

commands.desc: Quiet mode — exit 0 if found, 1 if not (no output)
commands.tooltip: Runs silently — prints nothing, but exits with code 0 if the pattern was found or 1 if not. Used in scripts to test if a pattern exists.

commands.desc: Replace first occurrence of 'old' with 'new' per line
commands.tooltip: Replaces the first occurrence of 'old' with 'new' on each line. sed is a 'stream editor' that processes files line by line.

commands.desc: Replace all occurrences of 'old' with 'new'
commands.tooltip: Replaces every occurrence of 'old' with 'new' on each line. The /g flag means 'global' (all occurrences, not just the first).

commands.desc: Edit file in-place replacing all occurrences
commands.tooltip: Edits the file directly on disk (in-place) instead of just printing the result. -i means 'in-place'. Without -i, sed only prints changes.

commands.desc: In-place edit with .bak backup created
commands.tooltip: Edits the file in-place AND creates a backup with .bak extension first, so you can undo if something goes wrong.

commands.desc: Print only lines 5 through 10
commands.tooltip: Prints only lines 5 through 10, suppressing all others. -n suppresses default printing; p means 'print these lines'.

commands.desc: Delete lines matching a pattern
commands.tooltip: Removes (deletes) any line that matches the pattern from the output.

commands.desc: Print only lines matching a pattern
commands.tooltip: Prints only lines that match the pattern, like grep but using sed's syntax.

commands.desc: Delete the first line of a file
commands.tooltip: Removes the first line of a file from the output — useful for skipping a header row.

commands.desc: Delete the last line of a file
commands.tooltip: Removes the last line. $ in sed means 'the last line'.

commands.desc: Prepend text to the beginning of each line
commands.tooltip: Adds text at the beginning of every line. ^ matches the start of each line.

commands.desc: Append text to the end of each line
commands.tooltip: Adds text at the end of every line. $ matches the end of each line.

commands.desc: Print file (suppress default, print explicitly)
commands.tooltip: Prints the file normally (suppress with -n, re-enable with p). Useful as a template for sed scripts.

commands.desc: Print the first field of each line (space-delimited)
commands.tooltip: awk is a powerful text-processing tool. $1 refers to the first field (column) of each line, split by spaces by default.

commands.desc: Print the last field of each line
commands.tooltip: $NF is awk's built-in variable for the last field — NF stands for 'Number of Fields'. Useful when the last column position varies.

commands.desc: Set colon as field separator and print first field
commands.tooltip: Tells awk to split each line using : as the field separator (-F), then prints the first field. Used to extract usernames from /etc/passwd.

commands.desc: Print only the 5th line
commands.tooltip: NR is awk's line counter ('Number of Records'). This prints only the 5th line.

commands.desc: Print lines 5 through 10
commands.tooltip: Prints lines 5 through 10 by checking awk's line counter (NR) against a range.

commands.desc: Print lines matching a pattern
commands.tooltip: Prints every line that matches a pattern — similar to grep but within awk, allowing more complex processing after matching.

commands.desc: Sum values in the first column
commands.tooltip: Adds up all the values in the first column and prints the total at the END of processing. END is an awk block that runs after all lines.

commands.desc: Print the total number of lines in a file
commands.tooltip: NR at the END block holds the total number of lines processed — a way to count lines using awk.

commands.desc: Print each line with its line number prepended
commands.tooltip: Prints each line prefixed with its line number by prepending NR to $0 (the whole line).

commands.desc: Print lines where the third field is greater than 100
commands.tooltip: Filters lines where the value in the third column is greater than 100 — awk can do numeric comparisons on fields.

commands.desc: Extract the second field from a comma-delimited file
commands.tooltip: Extracts the second column from a comma-separated file. -d',' sets comma as the field delimiter, -f2 selects field 2.

commands.desc: Extract characters 1 through 10 from each line
commands.tooltip: Extracts characters 1 through 10 from every line, by character position rather than by delimiter.

commands.desc: Sort lines of a file alphabetically
commands.tooltip: Sorts the lines of a file alphabetically (A to Z). The output is printed but the file itself is unchanged.

commands.desc: Sort lines in reverse order
commands.tooltip: Sorts lines in reverse alphabetical order (Z to A). -r means 'reverse'.

commands.desc: Sort lines numerically
commands.tooltip: Sorts lines treating them as numbers instead of text, so 10 comes after 9 (not before 2 as it would alphabetically).

commands.desc: Sort by the second field
commands.tooltip: Sorts by the second space-separated field (column) instead of the whole line.

commands.desc: Sort and remove duplicate lines
commands.tooltip: Sorts and removes duplicate lines, keeping only unique lines. -u means 'unique'.

commands.desc: Sort passwd file by UID (3rd colon-separated field)
commands.tooltip: Sorts the /etc/passwd file numerically by the third colon-separated field (which is the user ID number). -t: sets colon as separator.

commands.desc: Remove consecutive duplicate lines
commands.tooltip: Removes consecutive duplicate lines from the output. Only works on adjacent duplicates, so sort first if needed.

commands.desc: Prefix each unique line with its occurrence count
commands.tooltip: Counts how many times each repeated line appears, printing the count next to each line.

commands.desc: Print only duplicate (repeated) lines
commands.tooltip: Prints only lines that appear more than once (duplicated lines) in the input.

commands.desc: Print only unique (non-repeated) lines
commands.tooltip: Prints only lines that appear exactly once — lines without any duplicates.

commands.desc: Translate lowercase to uppercase from stdin
commands.tooltip: Translates (converts) every lowercase letter to its uppercase equivalent. Reads from the terminal (or a pipe) and outputs converted text.

commands.desc: Delete newline characters from stdin
commands.tooltip: Deletes all newline characters from the input, joining all lines into a single continuous string.

commands.desc: Squeeze repeated spaces into a single space
commands.tooltip: Replaces multiple consecutive spaces with a single space. -s means 'squeeze repeated characters'.

commands.desc: Delete all characters except alphanumerics
commands.tooltip: Keeps only letters and numbers, deleting everything else. -d means 'delete', -c means 'complement' (invert the set), so -dc keeps the set instead.

commands.desc: Build and execute command lines from stdin input
commands.tooltip: Passes each line from standard input as an argument to the specified command. Useful when a command doesn't accept piped input directly.

commands.desc: Use placeholder to pass each input as argument
commands.tooltip: Uses {} as a placeholder that gets replaced by each input item. This renames every file by adding a .bak extension.

commands.desc: Pass one argument at a time to the command
commands.tooltip: Passes one item at a time as an argument to the command, instead of all items at once.

commands.desc: Run command with 4 parallel processes
commands.tooltip: Runs the command in 4 parallel processes simultaneously, speeding up batch operations.

commands.desc: Align CSV columns into a readable table
commands.tooltip: Aligns columns in a CSV file into a readable table. -s',' sets comma as the column separator.

commands.desc: Merge lines of files side by side with tab separator
commands.tooltip: Merges two files side by side, joining each line of file1 with the corresponding line of file2 using a tab between them.

commands.desc: Merge files using comma as delimiter
commands.tooltip: Merges files line by line but uses a comma as the delimiter between them.

commands.desc: Join two sorted files on a common first field
commands.tooltip: Merges two sorted files based on matching values in their first column — like a database JOIN.

commands.desc: Compare two sorted files line by line (3 columns)
commands.tooltip: Compares two sorted files and shows three columns: lines only in file1, lines only in file2, and lines in both.

commands.desc: Show only lines common to both sorted files
commands.tooltip: Shows only the lines that appear in BOTH sorted files — the intersection of the two files.

commands.desc: Show word-level differences between two files
commands.tooltip: Highlights differences between two files at the word level rather than the line level — more granular than diff.

commands.desc: Extract human-readable strings from a binary
commands.tooltip: Extracts all printable text strings (at least 4 characters long) from a binary file like an executable.

commands.desc: Display the command history list
commands.tooltip: Shows a numbered list of all the commands you've previously run in this shell. Use the numbers with ! to re-run them.

commands.desc: Show the last 20 commands in history
commands.tooltip: Shows only the most recent 20 commands in your history.

commands.desc: Re-execute command number 42 from history
commands.tooltip: Re-runs a specific command by its history number — the ! followed by a number means 'execute command #42'.

commands.desc: Re-execute the last command
commands.tooltip: Re-runs your previous command. !! is a shortcut that expands to the last command. Useful for adding sudo: 'sudo !!'.

commands.desc: Re-execute the most recent command starting with 'string'
commands.tooltip: Re-runs the most recent command that starts with 'string'. For example, !git runs your last git command.

commands.desc: Clear the entire command history
commands.tooltip: Erases your entire command history. -c means 'clear'.

commands.desc: Reverse-search through command history interactively
commands.tooltip: Press Ctrl+R to search backward through your command history by typing part of an old command.

commands.desc: Create an alias 'll' for 'ls -la'
commands.tooltip: Creates a shortcut (alias) so that typing 'll' runs 'ls -la'. Aliases save typing for commands you use often.

commands.desc: List all defined aliases
commands.tooltip: Lists all currently defined aliases in your shell, showing what each shortcut expands to.

commands.desc: Remove the alias 'll'
commands.tooltip: Removes (deletes) the alias 'll' from the current shell session.

commands.desc: Reload the bash configuration file in current shell
commands.tooltip: Reloads your .bashrc configuration file so changes take effect immediately without opening a new terminal. source runs a script in the current shell.

commands.desc: Dot-source the bashrc file (same as source)
commands.tooltip: Same as 'source' — the dot (.) is shorthand for running a script in the current shell environment.

commands.desc: Set an environment variable and export to child processes
commands.tooltip: Creates an environment variable and makes it available to any programs you launch from this shell. 'Export' shares it with child processes.

commands.desc: Add a new directory to the PATH environment variable
commands.tooltip: Adds a new directory to the PATH, which is the list of places the shell looks for executable programs. $PATH:newdir appends to the existing list.

commands.desc: Display all environment variables
commands.tooltip: Displays all currently set environment variables — the settings and values that programs inherit from the shell.

commands.desc: Run a command with a specific environment variable set
commands.tooltip: Runs a command with a temporary environment variable that only applies to that one command, not your whole session.

commands.desc: Display all shell variables and functions
commands.tooltip: Shows all shell variables, functions, and settings — more comprehensive than env, which only shows exported variables.

commands.desc: Exit script immediately if any command returns non-zero
commands.tooltip: Makes the script exit immediately if any command fails (returns a non-zero exit code). A safety feature for scripts.

commands.desc: Treat unset variables as an error and exit
commands.tooltip: Treats any use of an undefined variable as an error and exits. Prevents bugs from typos in variable names.

commands.desc: Print each command and its arguments before executing (debug)
commands.tooltip: Prints each command before executing it — like a trace mode. Invaluable for debugging scripts to see exactly what's running.

commands.desc: Return non-zero if any command in a pipeline fails
commands.tooltip: Changes the behavior of pipelines (commands joined with |): the pipeline fails if ANY command in it fails, not just the last one.

commands.desc: Remove a variable or function from the environment
commands.tooltip: Removes (unsets) a variable or function so it no longer exists in the shell environment.

commands.desc: Declare an integer variable
commands.tooltip: Declares a variable that can only hold integers. -i means 'integer'. Arithmetic operations will work correctly on it.

commands.desc: Declare a read-only (constant) variable
commands.tooltip: Creates a read-only variable that cannot be changed or unset — like a constant. -r means 'readonly'.

commands.desc: Declare an indexed array
commands.tooltip: Creates an indexed array (like a list). Access elements with ${arr[0]}, ${arr[1]}, etc.

commands.desc: Declare an associative array (hash map)
commands.tooltip: Creates an associative array (like a dictionary/hash map) where keys are strings instead of numbers.

commands.desc: Mark variable for export to child processes
commands.tooltip: Marks a variable for automatic export so child processes inherit it, same as using export.

commands.desc: Create a read-only variable that cannot be changed
commands.tooltip: Creates a variable that cannot be modified. Similar to declare -r.

commands.desc: Declare a local variable inside a function
commands.tooltip: Creates a variable that only exists inside the function where it's declared — it won't interfere with variables outside.

commands.desc: Show how a name would be interpreted (alias, function, builtin, file)
commands.tooltip: Shows how the shell would interpret a name: is it an alias, a shell function, a shell builtin, or an external program?

commands.desc: Show the full path of a command in PATH
commands.tooltip: Searches your PATH and prints the full location of a command's executable file (e.g. /usr/bin/python3).

commands.desc: Locate the binary, source, and man page of a command
commands.tooltip: Finds the binary, source code, and manual page for a command — broader than 'which'.

commands.desc: Display a one-line description from the man page
commands.tooltip: Prints a one-line description of a command taken from its manual page.

commands.desc: Check if a command exists and show its path
commands.tooltip: Checks if a command exists and prints its path or type. Returns exit code 0 if found (useful in scripts).

commands.desc: Use the bash builtin version of a command, bypassing aliases
commands.tooltip: Forces use of the shell's built-in version of a command, bypassing any aliases or functions with the same name.

commands.desc: Enable ** glob to match files in all subdirectories
commands.tooltip: Enables the ** glob pattern, which matches files in all subdirectories. So **/*.py matches every Python file anywhere in the tree.

commands.desc: Allow globs to expand to empty string when no match
commands.tooltip: Allows glob patterns that match nothing to expand to an empty string instead of causing an error.

commands.desc: List all settable shell options and their current state
commands.tooltip: Lists all configurable shell options (like globstar, nullglob) and whether each is currently on or off.

commands.desc: List all readline key binding functions
commands.tooltip: Lists all keyboard shortcut functions available through readline (the library that handles command-line editing).

commands.desc: Bind Ctrl+F to move forward one word in readline
commands.tooltip: Creates a custom keyboard shortcut. This example makes Ctrl+F jump forward one word while typing a command.

commands.desc: Define tab-completion words for a command
commands.tooltip: Defines what words should appear as tab-completion options for a custom command.

commands.desc: List all commands available to the shell
commands.tooltip: Lists every command available in the current shell environment — all executables in your PATH plus builtins.

commands.desc: List all shell builtin commands
commands.tooltip: Lists only the commands built directly into bash (not external programs), like cd, echo, and read.

commands.desc: List all defined aliases
commands.tooltip: Lists all currently defined aliases — same output as running 'alias' with no arguments.

commands.desc: Run a function or command when the script exits
commands.tooltip: Sets up a cleanup action: when the script exits (for any reason), the specified function or command will run automatically.

commands.desc: Catch Ctrl+C (SIGINT) and run a handler
commands.tooltip: Catches the Ctrl+C interrupt signal (SIGINT) and runs a handler instead of immediately terminating the script.

commands.desc: Wait for all background jobs to finish
commands.tooltip: Pauses the current script until all background jobs it started have finished.

commands.desc: Wait for the last background process to finish
commands.tooltip: Waits specifically for the most recently started background process ($! holds its process ID) to finish.

commands.desc: Replace current shell process with a new bash instance
commands.tooltip: Replaces the current shell process with a new bash session. The current shell is gone; the new one takes its place.

commands.desc: Evaluate a string as a shell command
commands.tooltip: Evaluates a string as if it were a command you typed. Used to dynamically build and run commands.

commands.desc: Shift positional parameters left (drop $1, $2 becomes $1)
commands.tooltip: Removes the first positional parameter ($1), shifting all remaining ones down. $2 becomes $1, $3 becomes $2, etc.

commands.desc: Shift positional parameters left by 2 positions
commands.tooltip: Removes the first 2 positional parameters, shifting the rest down by 2 positions.

commands.desc: Parse short options in a shell script
commands.tooltip: Parses command-line options (like -a or -b value) in scripts. The colon after b means it expects a value.

commands.desc: Prompt the user and read input into a variable
commands.tooltip: Displays a prompt to the user and waits for them to type something, storing their input in the variable.

commands.desc: Read input silently (no echo) for passwords
commands.tooltip: Reads input without showing what the user types — used for passwords. -s means 'silent' (no echo).

commands.desc: Read whitespace-separated words into an array
commands.tooltip: Reads a line of space-separated words and stores each word as an element in an array.

commands.desc: Read file lines into an array variable
commands.tooltip: Reads every line of a file into an array, with each line becoming one array element. -t strips trailing newlines.

commands.desc: Print formatted output, safer than echo for special chars
commands.tooltip: Prints formatted output. Unlike echo, printf gives you precise control over formatting and handles special characters safely.

commands.desc: Echo with escape sequence interpretation enabled
commands.tooltip: Prints text and interprets escape sequences: \\n becomes a newline, \\t becomes a tab, etc. -e enables interpretation.

commands.desc: Echo without a trailing newline
commands.tooltip: Prints text without adding a newline at the end — useful when you want the cursor to stay on the same line.

commands.desc: Start a heredoc — read multiline text until EOF marker
commands.tooltip: Starts a 'here document' — a way to write multi-line text directly in a script until the EOF marker is reached.

commands.desc: Heredoc that strips leading tabs (allows indentation)
commands.tooltip: Like a heredoc but strips any leading tab characters from each line, allowing you to indent the content for readability.

commands.desc: Print the exit status of the last command
commands.tooltip: Shows the exit code of the last command: 0 means success, any other number means it failed or encountered an error.

commands.desc: Print the PID of the current shell process
commands.tooltip: Prints the Process ID (PID) of the current shell — every running program has a unique ID number.

commands.desc: Print the PID of the last background process
commands.tooltip: Prints the PID of the most recently started background process. Useful for tracking or waiting for it.

commands.desc: Use default value if VAR is unset or empty
commands.tooltip: Expands VAR if it has a value, or uses 'default' if VAR is unset or empty. A safe way to provide fallback values.

commands.desc: Print the length of a variable's value
commands.tooltip: Prints how many characters are in the variable's value. ${#VAR} is 'string length of VAR'.

commands.desc: Convert variable value to uppercase
commands.tooltip: Converts the variable's value to all uppercase letters.

commands.desc: Convert variable value to lowercase
commands.tooltip: Converts the variable's value to all lowercase letters.

commands.desc: Replace first occurrence in variable value
commands.tooltip: Replaces only the first occurrence of 'old' with 'new' within a variable's value.

commands.desc: Replace all occurrences in variable value
commands.tooltip: Replaces every occurrence of 'old' with 'new' within a variable's value. // means 'replace all'.

commands.desc: Redirect stdout to a file (overwrite)
commands.tooltip: Saves a command's output to a file, overwriting any existing content. > is the redirect-to-file operator.

commands.desc: Redirect stdout to a file (append)
commands.tooltip: Appends a command's output to a file without erasing what's already there. >> adds to the end.

commands.desc: Redirect stdin from a file
commands.tooltip: Feeds the contents of a file to a command as if you typed it. < is the redirect-from-file operator.

commands.desc: Redirect stderr to a file
commands.tooltip: Saves error messages (stderr, file descriptor 2) to a file separately from normal output.

commands.desc: Append stderr to a file
commands.tooltip: Appends error messages to a file instead of overwriting. >> for stderr.

commands.desc: Redirect stdout and stderr to separate files
commands.tooltip: Sends normal output to one file and error messages to a different file — keeps them separate for easier debugging.

commands.desc: Redirect stderr to the same destination as stdout
commands.tooltip: Merges error messages into the same stream as normal output. 2>&1 means 'send stderr (2) to wherever stdout (&1) goes'.

commands.desc: Redirect both stdout and stderr to the same file
commands.tooltip: Sends both normal output and error messages to the same file by first redirecting stdout to the file, then merging stderr into stdout.

commands.desc: Redirect both stdout and stderr to a file (bash shorthand)
commands.tooltip: Bash shorthand for redirecting both stdout and stderr to a file at once.

commands.desc: Discard stdout output (send to null device)
commands.tooltip: /dev/null is a special 'black hole' — anything sent there is discarded instantly. Useful for silencing noisy commands.

commands.desc: Discard stderr output silently
commands.tooltip: Discards only error messages, letting normal output through. Useful for hiding 'Permission denied' messages.

commands.desc: Discard all output (stdout and stderr)
commands.tooltip: Discards all output — both normal output and errors — completely silencing the command.

commands.desc: Pipe stdout of cmd1 as stdin to cmd2
commands.tooltip: Connects two commands: the output of cmd1 becomes the input of cmd2. Pipes let you chain tools together.

commands.desc: Tee output — write to file and continue pipeline
commands.tooltip: Splits the pipeline: writes the data to a file AND passes it along to the next command. tee is like a T-junction in plumbing.

commands.desc: Pipe both stdout and stderr of cmd1 to cmd2
commands.tooltip: Pipes both stdout and error messages from cmd1 into cmd2, so cmd2 sees everything.

commands.desc: Process substitution — use command output as a file
commands.tooltip: Process substitution: runs cmd1 and cmd2, treating their outputs as if they were files. diff can then compare them.

commands.desc: Process substitution — pipe stdout into another command
commands.tooltip: Another form of process substitution: sends stdout into cmd2 as if it were a file opened for writing.

commands.desc: Use xargs to pass file lines as arguments to grep
commands.tooltip: Passes each line of a file as a separate argument to grep, using xargs to bridge the pipe.

commands.desc: Open file descriptor 3 for writing
commands.tooltip: Opens a file for writing on file descriptor 3. File descriptors are numbered channels for input/output (0=stdin, 1=stdout, 2=stderr).

commands.desc: Close file descriptor 3
commands.tooltip: Closes file descriptor 3. The >&- syntax means 'close this descriptor'.

commands.desc: Set file permissions to rwxr-xr-x (owner full, others read/exec)
commands.tooltip: Sets who can read (r), write (w), and execute (x) a file. 755 means: owner gets all permissions, group and others get read+execute.

commands.desc: Set file permissions to rw-r--r-- (owner rw, others read)
commands.tooltip: Sets permissions to 644: the owner can read and write, everyone else can only read. A common setting for regular files.

commands.desc: Add execute permission for all users
commands.tooltip: Adds the 'execute' permission for all users, allowing the file to be run as a program. + means 'add permission'.

commands.desc: Remove execute permission for all users
commands.tooltip: Removes the execute permission from all users. - means 'remove permission'.

commands.desc: Add execute permission for the file owner only
commands.tooltip: Adds execute permission for the file's owner only. u stands for 'user' (the file owner).

commands.desc: Add write permission for the group
commands.tooltip: Adds write permission for the file's group. g stands for 'group'.

commands.desc: Remove read permission for others
commands.tooltip: Removes read permission for 'others' (everyone who isn't the owner or in the group). o stands for 'others'.

commands.desc: Add read permission for all (user, group, others)
commands.tooltip: Adds read permission for all three categories: user (owner), group, and others. a stands for 'all'.

commands.desc: Recursively set permissions on a directory
commands.tooltip: Applies permission changes to a directory and everything inside it recursively. -R means 'recursive'.

commands.desc: Set permissions using symbolic notation explicitly
commands.tooltip: Sets permissions using explicit notation: owner gets rwx, group gets rx, others get r. Clearer than numeric notation.

commands.desc: Change the owner of a file
commands.tooltip: Changes the owner of a file. Only root (the system administrator) can change file ownership.

commands.desc: Change both owner and group of a file
commands.tooltip: Changes both the owner and the group of a file in one command. The format is user:group.

commands.desc: Recursively change owner and group of a directory
commands.tooltip: Recursively changes ownership of a directory and all files inside it. -R means 'recursive'.

commands.desc: Change the group ownership of a file
commands.tooltip: Changes only the group ownership of a file, leaving the owner unchanged.

commands.desc: Recursively change group ownership
commands.tooltip: Recursively changes the group of a directory and all its contents.

commands.desc: Show the current file creation mask
commands.tooltip: Shows the current umask — a mask that determines the default permissions for newly created files and directories.

commands.desc: Set umask so new files get 644 and directories get 755
commands.tooltip: Sets the umask so new files get permissions 644 (rw-r--r--) and new directories get 755 (rwxr-xr-x).

commands.desc: Set umask so only owner can read/write new files
commands.tooltip: Sets a restrictive umask: only the owner can read and write new files; group and others get no access.

commands.desc: Run a command as root (superuser)
commands.tooltip: Runs a command with root (administrator) privileges. sudo stands for 'superuser do'. You'll be prompted for your password.

commands.desc: Run a command as a specific user
commands.tooltip: Runs a command as a specific user (not necessarily root). Useful for testing what another user can do.

commands.desc: Start an interactive root login shell
commands.tooltip: Opens a full root login shell — you become root with root's environment. Be careful with root access.

commands.desc: Start a root shell preserving current environment
commands.tooltip: Opens a root shell but keeps your current environment variables. Like sudo -i but preserves your settings.

commands.desc: Re-run the last command with sudo
commands.tooltip: Re-runs your last command with sudo prepended. !! expands to the previous command — useful when you forget sudo.

commands.desc: Switch to root user (interactive login)
commands.tooltip: Switches to the root user account. You'll need root's password (not your own).

commands.desc: Switch to another user with their login environment
commands.tooltip: Switches to another user and loads their full login environment (shell settings, PATH, etc.).

commands.desc: Safely edit the sudoers file with syntax validation
commands.tooltip: Opens the sudoers file (which controls who can use sudo) with syntax checking to prevent configuration errors.

commands.desc: Display current user's UID, GID, and group memberships
commands.tooltip: Shows your user ID (UID), primary group ID (GID), and all the groups you belong to.

commands.desc: Print the current user's username
commands.tooltip: Prints just your username — the account you're currently logged in as.

commands.desc: List all groups the current user belongs to
commands.tooltip: Lists all the groups your account belongs to. Group membership controls access to certain files and resources.

commands.desc: Get extended access control list (ACL) of a file
commands.tooltip: Shows the extended Access Control List (ACL) for a file — more granular permissions beyond the basic owner/group/others.

commands.desc: Grant user alice read/write ACL on a file
commands.tooltip: Grants a specific user (alice) read and write access to a file using ACLs, without changing regular permissions.

commands.desc: Remove ACL entry for user alice from a file
commands.tooltip: Removes the ACL entry specifically for user alice from a file.

commands.desc: List extended file attributes (immutable, append-only, etc.)
commands.tooltip: Shows special file attributes like 'immutable' (cannot be changed by anyone) or 'append-only'.

commands.desc: Set immutable attribute — file cannot be modified or deleted
commands.tooltip: Sets the immutable flag on a file — even root cannot modify or delete it until the flag is removed.

commands.desc: Remove immutable attribute from a file
commands.tooltip: Removes the immutable flag, allowing the file to be modified or deleted again.

commands.desc: Create a new user account named alice
commands.tooltip: Creates a new user account. This only creates the account; it does NOT create a home directory by default.

commands.desc: Create new user and create their home directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a new user AND creates their home directory (e.g. /home/alice). -m means 'make home directory'.

commands.desc: Create user with home directory and bash as default shell
commands.tooltip: Creates a new user with a home directory and sets bash as their default shell (the program that interprets their commands).

commands.desc: Create user and add to supplementary groups
commands.tooltip: Creates a new user and adds them to multiple groups. Being in 'sudo' allows admin commands; 'docker' allows Docker access.

commands.desc: Create user with a specific UID
commands.tooltip: Creates a user with a specific user ID number instead of the next available one.

commands.desc: Add user alice to the docker group without removing existing groups
commands.tooltip: Adds an existing user to the docker group without removing them from any other groups. -a means 'append' (don't replace existing groups).

commands.desc: Change alice's default shell to zsh
commands.tooltip: Changes alice's login shell to zsh. The shell is the program that interprets commands when she logs in.

commands.desc: Rename user alice to newname
commands.tooltip: Renames a user account. Only changes the login name, not the home directory.

commands.desc: Lock alice's password (prevent login)
commands.tooltip: Locks alice's account by disabling her password. She cannot log in until unlocked.

commands.desc: Unlock alice's password
commands.tooltip: Unlocks alice's account, re-enabling password login.

commands.desc: Delete user alice (home directory kept)
commands.tooltip: Deletes a user account but keeps their files in their home directory.

commands.desc: Delete user alice and remove their home directory
commands.tooltip: Deletes a user account AND removes their home directory and files. -r means 'remove home directory'.

commands.desc: Change the current user's password
commands.tooltip: Prompts you to set a new password for your own account.

commands.desc: Change password for user alice (as root)
commands.tooltip: Lets root (admin) change the password for any user account.

commands.desc: Lock alice's account by disabling their password
commands.tooltip: Locks an account by disabling the password — the user cannot log in with a password.

commands.desc: Unlock alice's account
commands.tooltip: Unlocks a user's password, allowing password-based login again.

commands.desc: Force alice to change their password at next login
commands.tooltip: Forces the user to change their password the very next time they log in. -e means 'expire immediately'.

commands.desc: Create a new group named developers
commands.tooltip: Creates a new group. Groups are used to give multiple users shared access to files.

commands.desc: Rename a group from developers to newname
commands.tooltip: Renames a group from 'developers' to 'newname'. Members remain in the group.

commands.desc: Delete the developers group
commands.tooltip: Deletes a group. This does not delete the users in it.

commands.desc: Add alice to the developers group
commands.tooltip: Adds alice to the developers group using the group administrator tool (gpasswd).

commands.desc: Remove alice from the developers group
commands.tooltip: Removes alice from the developers group.

commands.desc: Show who is currently logged into the system
commands.tooltip: Shows who is currently logged into the system and on which terminal.

commands.desc: Show when the system was last booted
commands.tooltip: Shows the date and time the system was last started (booted).

commands.desc: Show logged-in users and their current activity
commands.tooltip: Shows currently logged-in users along with what they're running and how long they've been idle.

commands.desc: Show history of user logins and system reboots
commands.tooltip: Shows a log of recent user logins and system reboots, most recent first.

commands.desc: Show the last 20 login records
commands.tooltip: Shows the 20 most recent login records from the system log.

commands.desc: Show the most recent login of all users
commands.tooltip: Shows the most recent login time for every user account on the system.

commands.desc: Display information about a user including login details
commands.tooltip: Displays information about a user — full name, home directory, login shell, and recent login time.

commands.desc: Start a new shell with developers as the effective group
commands.tooltip: Starts a new shell session with 'developers' as your active group, giving you that group's permissions temporarily.

commands.desc: List processes running in the current shell session
commands.tooltip: Lists only the processes running in your current terminal session. ps stands for 'process status'.

commands.desc: List all processes for all users with detailed info
commands.tooltip: Lists every running process on the system for all users. a=all users, u=user-friendly format, x=include processes without a terminal.

commands.desc: Find all processes matching nginx
commands.tooltip: Combines ps aux with grep to find processes whose name or command contains 'nginx'. A common way to check if a service is running.

commands.desc: Full format listing of all processes
commands.tooltip: Lists all processes in a full format with parent process IDs (PPIDs), showing the process hierarchy.

commands.desc: Custom process listing with specific columns
commands.tooltip: Shows a custom selection of columns: process ID, parent ID, command, CPU%, and memory%. Useful for monitoring specific metrics.

commands.desc: List processes sorted by CPU usage (highest first)
commands.tooltip: Lists processes sorted from highest to lowest CPU usage. head limits the output to the top 10.

commands.desc: List processes sorted by memory usage (highest first)
commands.tooltip: Lists processes sorted from highest to lowest memory usage. Helps identify memory-hungry programs.

commands.desc: Show information for a specific process by PID
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed information about a single specific process identified by its PID (Process ID).

commands.desc: Interactive real-time view of system processes and resource usage
commands.tooltip: Opens a live, continuously updating view of all running processes sorted by CPU usage. Press q to quit.

commands.desc: Show only processes belonging to user alice in top
commands.tooltip: Filters top to only show processes belonging to user alice.

commands.desc: Monitor a specific process by PID in top
commands.tooltip: Monitors a single specific process in top, identified by its PID.

commands.desc: Run top with a 2-second refresh interval
commands.tooltip: Runs top and refreshes every 2 seconds instead of the default 3 seconds.

commands.desc: Run top in batch mode for one iteration (scriptable)
commands.tooltip: Runs top without interactive display — useful for scripts that need to capture process stats.

commands.desc: Interactive process viewer with a better UI than top
commands.tooltip: A more user-friendly alternative to top with color, mouse support, and easier controls. Press F1 for help.

commands.desc: Show only processes for user alice in htop
commands.tooltip: Shows only processes belonging to alice in the htop display.

commands.desc: Send SIGTERM signal to process with PID 1234 (graceful stop)
commands.tooltip: Sends a graceful stop request to a process. The process can clean up before stopping. If it ignores this, use -9.

commands.desc: Send SIGKILL to process 1234 (force kill)
commands.tooltip: Forcefully kills a process immediately — it cannot be caught or ignored. Use as a last resort when the process won't stop normally.

commands.desc: Send SIGHUP to process 1234 (reload config)
commands.tooltip: Sends the HUP (hangup) signal, which many daemons interpret as 'reload your configuration without restarting'.

commands.desc: List all available signal names and numbers
commands.tooltip: Lists all signal names and their numbers. Signals are messages you can send to processes to control their behavior.

commands.desc: Kill processes by name
commands.tooltip: Kills all processes with the name 'nginx' — finds them by name rather than requiring you to know their PID.

commands.desc: Force kill all processes matching name nginx
commands.tooltip: Forcefully kills all processes matching the name 'nginx' without giving them a chance to clean up.

commands.desc: Kill all processes owned by user alice
commands.tooltip: Kills all processes owned by user alice in one command.

commands.desc: Kill all processes named exactly nginx
commands.tooltip: Kills all processes with the exact name 'nginx'. Similar to pkill but requires an exact name match.

commands.desc: Start a command with lower priority (nice value 10)
commands.tooltip: Starts a command with a lower scheduling priority. Nice values range from -20 (highest) to 19 (lowest). Higher nice = lower priority.

commands.desc: Start a command with higher priority (needs root)
commands.tooltip: Starts a command with higher-than-normal priority (needs root). Negative nice values mean higher priority.

commands.desc: Change the priority of running process 1234
commands.tooltip: Changes the priority of an already-running process. Higher nice value = lower priority = friendlier to other processes.

commands.desc: Run command immune to hangups, output to nohup.out
commands.tooltip: Runs a command immune to the 'hangup' signal (which is sent when you close your terminal). The command keeps running even after you log out.

commands.desc: Run command in background, redirect all output to log
commands.tooltip: Runs a command in the background that survives terminal closure, with all output saved to a log file.

commands.desc: Resume the most recent suspended job in the background
commands.tooltip: Resumes the most recently suspended job and runs it in the background. Use Ctrl+Z to suspend a job first.

commands.desc: Resume job number 2 in the background
commands.tooltip: Resumes a specific numbered job in the background. Use 'jobs' to see job numbers.

commands.desc: Bring the most recent background job to the foreground
commands.tooltip: Brings the most recently backgrounded job to the foreground so you can interact with it.

commands.desc: Bring job number 2 to the foreground
commands.tooltip: Brings job number 2 to the foreground. Use 'jobs' to see which number corresponds to which program.

commands.desc: List all active background jobs in the current shell
commands.tooltip: Lists all jobs running or suspended in the background of your current shell session.

commands.desc: List background jobs with their PIDs
commands.tooltip: Lists background jobs along with their Process IDs (PIDs).

commands.desc: Remove job 1 from the jobs table (survives shell exit)
commands.tooltip: Detaches job 1 from the shell so it won't be killed when the shell exits, without sending it to the background.

commands.desc: Mark job 1 to ignore SIGHUP without removing from table
commands.tooltip: Marks job 1 to ignore the hangup signal so it keeps running after logout, but keeps it in the jobs table.

commands.desc: Wait for process 1234 to complete
commands.tooltip: Pauses the script until process 1234 finishes. Used to synchronize background tasks.

commands.desc: Find PIDs of processes matching a name
commands.tooltip: Searches for running processes by name and prints their PIDs (Process IDs).

commands.desc: Find PIDs of all processes owned by alice
commands.tooltip: Finds the PIDs of all processes owned by the user alice.

commands.desc: Display running processes as a tree showing parent-child relations
commands.tooltip: Shows all running processes in a tree view, making parent-child relationships (which process launched which) visible.

commands.desc: Display process tree with PIDs shown
commands.tooltip: Shows the process tree with each process's PID displayed next to its name.

commands.desc: List all open files and the processes using them
commands.tooltip: Lists every open file on the system along with which process has it open. lsof stands for 'List Open Files'.

commands.desc: List files opened by user alice
commands.tooltip: Lists all files currently opened by user alice across all processes.

commands.desc: List files opened by process 1234
commands.tooltip: Lists all files opened by the process with PID 1234.

commands.desc: List processes using port 80
commands.tooltip: Lists which process is using port 80 — useful for finding what's listening on a network port.

commands.desc: Show PIDs of processes using port 80/tcp
commands.tooltip: Shows the PIDs of processes using TCP port 80. fuser identifies processes by their resource usage.

commands.desc: Kill all processes using port 80/tcp
commands.tooltip: Sends a kill signal to every process using TCP port 80 — useful for freeing a port that's stuck in use.

commands.desc: Trace system calls and signals made by a command
commands.tooltip: Monitors a command and prints every system call it makes (requests to the OS kernel). Invaluable for debugging.

commands.desc: Attach strace to a running process
commands.tooltip: Attaches strace to a process that's already running to start monitoring its system calls.

commands.desc: Trace library calls made by a program
commands.tooltip: Traces library function calls (like calls to libc functions) made by a program.

commands.desc: Measure real, user, and system time for a command
commands.tooltip: Measures how long a command takes to run, reporting the real elapsed time, CPU user time, and kernel time.

commands.desc: Run command and kill it if it runs longer than 30 seconds
commands.tooltip: Runs a command but kills it automatically if it hasn't finished within 30 seconds.

commands.desc: Run a command every 2 seconds and display its output
commands.tooltip: Runs a command repeatedly every 2 seconds, showing the refreshed output. Press Ctrl+C to stop. -n sets the interval.

commands.desc: Schedule a command to run at 2:00 PM
commands.tooltip: Schedules a one-time command to run at a specific future time. You'll be prompted to enter the command.

commands.desc: List pending at jobs
commands.tooltip: Lists all jobs scheduled with the 'at' command that haven't run yet.

commands.desc: Remove at job number 3
commands.tooltip: Cancels a scheduled 'at' job by its job number (shown by atq).

commands.desc: Schedule a command to run when system load is low
commands.tooltip: Queues a command to run when the system's CPU load drops below a threshold level — for low-priority background work.

commands.desc: Print all system information (kernel, hostname, arch, etc.)
commands.tooltip: Prints everything about the system: kernel name and version, hostname, hardware architecture, and OS. -a means 'all'.

commands.desc: Print the kernel release version
commands.tooltip: Prints just the kernel version number — the core of the operating system. Useful for checking if you need to update.

commands.desc: Print the machine hardware architecture
commands.tooltip: Prints the hardware architecture (e.g. x86_64 for 64-bit Intel/AMD, aarch64 for ARM). Tells you what type of CPU you have.

commands.desc: Display the system's hostname
commands.tooltip: Shows the machine's hostname — its name on the network. Like a computer's name on a local network.

commands.desc: Display all IP addresses of the machine
commands.tooltip: Shows all IP addresses assigned to the machine's network interfaces — useful for finding your local network address.

commands.desc: Show or set the system hostname and related settings
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed system identity information (hostname, machine ID, OS) and lets you change the hostname.

commands.desc: Show how long the system has been running and load average
commands.tooltip: Shows how long the system has been running since last boot, plus load averages for the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes.

commands.desc: Show uptime in human-readable format (e.g., up 3 days)
commands.tooltip: Shows system uptime in a human-friendly format like 'up 3 days, 2 hours, 15 minutes'.

commands.desc: Display the current date and time
commands.tooltip: Shows the current date and time according to the system clock.

commands.desc: Display date in a custom format
commands.tooltip: Shows the date and time in a custom format. %Y=4-digit year, %m=month, %d=day, %H=hour, %M=minute, %S=second.

commands.desc: Display yesterday's date
commands.tooltip: Shows yesterday's date — useful in scripts for generating dated filenames for logs.

commands.desc: Display a calendar for the current month
commands.tooltip: Displays a text calendar for the current month.

commands.desc: Display a full year calendar
commands.tooltip: Displays a calendar for an entire year, showing all 12 months.

commands.desc: List detailed hardware configuration of the machine
commands.tooltip: Lists all hardware in the system with detailed technical information about each component.

commands.desc: List hardware in compact summary format
commands.tooltip: Lists hardware in a brief, easy-to-scan table format instead of the full verbose output.

commands.desc: Display detailed CPU architecture information
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed information about the CPU: model, number of cores, cache sizes, and supported features.

commands.desc: List block devices (disks and partitions) in a tree
commands.tooltip: Lists all block devices (hard drives, SSDs, USB drives, partitions) in a tree view. Block devices are storage units.

commands.desc: List block devices with filesystem type and UUID
commands.tooltip: Lists block devices along with their filesystem type (like ext4 or vfat) and UUID (unique identifier).

commands.desc: List all PCI devices
commands.tooltip: Lists all PCI devices — things connected to the motherboard's PCI bus like graphics cards and network adapters.

commands.desc: List PCI devices with verbose details
commands.tooltip: Lists PCI devices with detailed technical information about each device.

commands.desc: List all USB devices
commands.tooltip: Lists all USB devices currently connected to the system.

commands.desc: List USB devices with verbose details
